items.question_text: Complete: Under 1-2-1i, "approved separation" means separation in accordance with the applicable _____ in this order.
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1i, approved separation is defined as separation in accordance with the applicable minima in this order — not any separation the controller believes is safe.

choices.text: minima

items.question_text: Under 1-2-1k, when is "miles" interpreted as statute miles rather than nautical miles?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1k, miles means nautical miles unless otherwise specified, and means statute miles in conjunction with visibility. This dual meaning is a common source of confusion.

choices.text: Whenever the term appears in a separation paragraph.
In conjunction with visibility.
Whenever the term is written in lowercase rather than all capitals.
Only when preceded by an altitude value.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-1l, course, bearing, azimuth, heading, and wind direction information must always be _____ unless specifically stated otherwise.
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1l, all course and directional information defaults to magnetic. The word 'magnetic' is the universal default that applies unless the order explicitly states otherwise.

choices.text: magnetic

items.question_text: Under 1-2-1m, when does the change to the next minute occur for ATC operational time?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1m, the change to the next minute is made at the minute plus 30 seconds. Time checks are an exception — they are given to the nearest quarter minute.

choices.text: At exactly the top of the minute.
At the minute plus 30 seconds.
At the minute plus 15 seconds.
At the minute plus 45 seconds.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-1m, how are time checks given, and how does this differ from the standard time convention for ATC operational activities?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1m, standard ATC time changes at the minute + 30 seconds, but time checks are given to the nearest quarter minute. This is an explicit exception to the normal time convention.

choices.text: Time checks are given to the nearest quarter minute, while standard ATC time changes at the minute plus 30 seconds.
Time checks use the same minute-plus-30 convention as all other ATC operational time.
Time checks are given to the nearest whole minute only.
Time checks are given in local time, not UTC.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-1n, a procedure references "the runway." The facility has both a concrete runway and a helipad with a takeoff course. Does the procedure apply to the helipad unless otherwise specified?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1n, 'runway' does not include helipads and/or their accompanying takeoff/landing courses unless otherwise specified. The default excludes helipads.

choices.text: No; runway does not include helipads or their takeoff/landing courses unless otherwise specified.
Yes; helipads are a type of runway and are always included in the term.
Yes, but only if the helipad has a paved surface.
It depends on whether the helipad is co-located on the same physical surface as the runway.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 1-2-2a, same courses have an angular difference of less than _____ degrees.
items.explanation: Per 1-2-2a, same courses have an angular difference of less than 45 degrees. This is the narrowest course classification.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 1-2-2c, opposite/reciprocal courses have an angular difference greater than _____ degrees through 180 degrees inclusive.
items.explanation: Per 1-2-2c, opposite/reciprocal courses are greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive. 135 degrees is the boundary — it belongs to crossing courses, not opposite.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-2, what condition must also be met beyond angular difference for courses to be classified as same or opposite/reciprocal?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-2a and 1-2-2c, in addition to the angular thresholds, same and opposite/reciprocal courses require that protected airspaces are coincident, overlap, or intersect. Angular difference alone is not sufficient.

choices.text: Only the angular difference matters; no additional condition is specified.
Protected airspaces must be coincident, overlap, or intersect.
The aircraft must be at the same altitude.
The courses must be within the same facility's airspace.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-2, two aircraft have intersecting protected airspaces with an angular difference of exactly 135 degrees. How are they classified?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-2b, crossing courses include 45 through 135 degrees inclusive. So 135 degrees is a crossing course. Opposite/reciprocal begins above 135 degrees.

choices.text: Opposite/reciprocal courses.
Crossing courses.
Same courses.
The classification is ambiguous because 135 is a boundary value.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 1-2-5c, bold vertical lines in the margin of changed pages indicate the location of _____ revisions to the order.
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5c, bold vertical lines mark substantive revisions. The word 'substantive' distinguishes these from minor formatting changes.

choices.text: substantive

items.question_text: Under 1-2-5c, what does a bold vertical line adjacent to the title of a chapter, section, or paragraph indicate that differs from a bold line in the margin adjacent to body text?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5c, bold lines adjacent to body text indicate individual substantive revisions, while bold lines adjacent to a title mean extensive changes have been made to that entire chapter, section, or paragraph.

choices.text: There is no difference; both indicate the same level of revision.
Adjacent to a title, it means extensive changes have been made to that chapter, section, or paragraph.
Adjacent to a title, it means the title text itself was changed.
Adjacent to a title, it means only the numbering was changed, not the content.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-5d, a paragraph is annotated TERMINAL. An en route controller reads it and wonders if it applies. What does the annotation mean?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5d, paragraphs annotated with EN ROUTE, OCEANIC, or TERMINAL are only to be applied by the designated type facility. When not so designated, they apply to all facility types.

choices.text: The paragraph applies only to terminal facilities; the en route controller does not apply it.
The paragraph applies to all facilities, but terminal facilities have priority.
The paragraph applies to all facilities that have a terminal radar approach function.
The annotation is advisory only and does not restrict application.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-5h, an EXAMPLE is provided but the preceding paragraph does not include specific prescribed phraseology. How must a controller treat the example text?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5h and its note, when the preceding paragraph lacks specific prescribed phraseology, the example text is not mandatory verbatim, but controllers are expected to approximate the wording to the extent practical.

choices.text: The exact text is mandatory and must be used verbatim.
The exact text is not mandatory, but the wording should approximate the example to the extent practical.
The example may be ignored entirely since no phraseology is prescribed.
The example applies only when the paragraph is also annotated TERMINAL.

items.situation: A controller reads a paragraph that says controllers 'will issue a clearance' and interprets 'will' as mandatory. A supervisor says 'will' means the action is mandatory. The controller re-reads 1-2-1 and sees that 'will' means futurity, not a requirement.
items.question_text: Under 1-2-1e, does 'will' in this order mean a procedure is mandatory?

choices.text: No; 'will' means futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure. 'Must' is the term that means mandatory.
Yes; 'will' and 'must' are interchangeable in this order.
Yes; 'will' always means mandatory in FAA publications.
It depends on whether the paragraph is annotated TERMINAL.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-1e, 'will' means futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure. This is one of the most commonly misunderstood definitions in the order.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-1h, what does the term 'aircraft' mean in this order?

choices.text: The airframe only.
The airframe, crew members, or both.
The crew members only.
The airframe and crew members together, never separately.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-1h, 'aircraft' means the airframe, crew members, or both. This inclusive definition means a reference to the aircraft can apply to any of these three possibilities.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-1o, how many distinct conditions must a state aircraft commander satisfy (choose at least one) to operate under due regard or operational options?

choices.text: Two conditions: separate from all other traffic AND assure search and rescue monitoring.
Four conditions: the first two obligations PLUS operate under at least one of four operational conditions.
One condition: operate under any of the listed conditions.
Three conditions: VMC, surveillance coverage, and Class G airspace.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-1o, the state aircraft commander has two mandatory obligations (separate from traffic, assure SAR monitoring) AND must operate under at least one of four conditions: VMC, ATC surveillance + communications, airborne radar, or Class G airspace.

items.situation: Two aircraft are on courses whose protected airspaces intersect. The angular difference between their courses is approximately 30 degrees. A trainee wants to classify them as crossing courses because the paths intersect.
items.question_text: Under 1-2-2, what is the correct course classification for intersecting protected airspaces with a 30-degree angular difference?

choices.text: Same courses, because the angular difference is less than 45 degrees and the protected airspaces intersect.
Crossing courses, because any intersecting paths are crossing courses.
Opposite/reciprocal courses, because the angle is too small to be crossing.
No classification applies because the protected airspaces also need to overlap in a specific way.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-2a, same courses are courses whose protected airspaces are coincident, overlap, or intersect and whose angular difference is less than 45 degrees. Intersection at 30 degrees satisfies both conditions for same courses.

items.question_text: Under the 1-2-2 note, what does it mean if the protected airspace of two aircraft does not overlap?

choices.text: Applicable lateral separation is ensured.
The aircraft must be on crossing courses.
Vertical separation must immediately be applied.
The course classification defaults to opposite/reciprocal.

items.explanation: Per the 1-2-2 note, if the protected airspace of two aircraft does not overlap, applicable lateral separation is ensured. This is a key shortcut for the controller — no need to classify the course relationship.

items.situation: A paragraph annotated USAF describes a military procedure. An Army controller at a joint-use facility reads it and assumes it also applies to Army aircraft since they are also military.
items.question_text: Does a paragraph annotated USAF apply to Army aircraft under 1-2-5e?

choices.text: No; USAF annotation means the procedure applies only to the U.S. Air Force.
Yes; all military annotations apply to any U.S. military service.
Yes, but only at joint-use facilities.
It depends on whether the Army facility has a letter of agreement with the Air Force unit.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-5e, USAF denotes the procedure applies only to the U.S. Air Force. USN applies only to Navy, and USA only to Army. Each service annotation is exclusive to that service.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-5, a paragraph contains the annotation WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION. What does this mean?

choices.text: Only the sentence immediately following the annotation requires wake turbulence procedures.
The remaining information in the paragraph requires the application of wake turbulence procedures.
The entire section requires wake turbulence procedures for all paragraphs within it.
Wake turbulence procedures are recommended but not required for that paragraph.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-5f, WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION inserted within a paragraph means the remaining information in the paragraph — not just the next sentence — requires wake turbulence procedures.
